body: This problem is very important because it causes programs that would otherwise work to be unavailable. These programs cannot be set to `proxy: false`
author: wll8

body: They cannot be set `proxy: false`, but is the proxy config specified (`proxy: { url: ... }`)?
author: arthurfiorette

body:
Any node.js (axios) program you don't write yourself is a black box to you, don't try to know or modify it.

In the best case, if axios supports proxies, then axios should work properly when proxies are available.
author: wll8

body:
So, this isn't a bug. You want a feature to detect proxies automatically. I don't think this will be done (maybe as an external plug-in)

Teorically, this should be something to put on a configuration file. But fortunately, it is a javascript project, you can just edit the source code.

@jasonsaayman is it possible to detect proxies automagically?

Even if it is, i don't think this should be included in axios by default.
author: arthurfiorette

body:
The original meaning is: There is a program written using axios because the system has set up a proxy, so it cannot work. It must be set axios proxy=false.

What do you mean, when the system sets an available proxy, and axios enables the use of the proxy by default, but cannot use the proxy normally, this is not a problem of axios?
author: wll8

body: My understanding is that when the system proxy is set, if both httpie and git can use this proxy normally, axios should also be able to, and even if it can't, it shouldn't cause the request to fail.
author: wll8

body:
Oh, so the proxy is configured on the system?
In the code, the proxy config is defined, undefined or false?

You had to set it false to make it work?

If I understood your problem, this might help:
https://github.com/wwwzbwcom/node-global-proxy
author: arthurfiorette

body:
- The `system` is set up with a proxy
- No proxy settings were made in the original code, this may be `undefined`
- axios must be set to `proxy:false` to work

Thanks, I am currently using: [https-proxy-agent](https://github.com/TooTallNate/node-https-proxy-agent)
author: wll8

body:
Oh maybe the original source already has another proxy configuration, and that's why you need to override the option with false.

Anyways, if `https-proxy-agent` is working, can you post here an example for future people with similar problems?

If not, it is hard to diagnose without code. So, can you setup an small code example that i can test myself?
author: arthurfiorette

body: There is a code example `t.js` at the beginning of [this post](https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/4369#issue-1093095680), you can try to see if it can be reproduced.

Well, i coulnd't reproduce it. Without forcing `proxy: false`, can you do the request and send the resolved configuration? (`response.config`)

And, can you paste the code you used with `https-proxy-agent`? In the future other people can have this same error.

body:
I have tried but failed to replicate the error.

I've created the http client with an 80000ms timeout.
```
 const httpClient = axios.create()
 httpClient.defaults.timeout = 80000
```

On the backend, I forced the server to wait 70000ms before responding.
```
await new Promise(res => {
    const timeout = setTimeout(() => {
       res()
     clearTimeout(timeout)
   }, 70000)
 })
```
The axios client responds normally after 70000ms. It also works if I change the timeout to 100000ms and the server time to 80000ms or 90000ms.

You've mentioned an 504 error on the expected behavior section. Maybe there in a 60000ms timeout on the server side ?

body: This problem appears to be due to someone recklessly optimizing the `isFormData` function in the utils.js file. This could break form detection logic in a non-browser environment where FormData is not a native object that can be recognized by the `Object.prototype.toString` method. Fixed in #4413 with some additional benefits.

body:
This is a shorter form, since it is still needed in 0.26.1

```ts
axios.post(url, formData, {
  headers: { 'Content-Type': 'multipart/form-data' },
  transformRequest: formData => formData,
})
```

The headers value might only be needed if you have modified the default Content-Type for axios. (just a guess since I have read that axios should set the correct Content-Type based on the FormData type)

In which axios version will the fix that closed this issue be released?

But after quite a while I have found a solution to fix this issue, the key is **don't set "Conten-type: multipart/form-data" manually, just remove it, let axios create that header automatically**. The folowing code will fix this issue :
> 
> ```
> const formData = new FormData();
> ...
> const response = await axios.post('/URL', formData, {
>   transformRequest: () => formData,
> });
> ```
> 
> Env: "axios": "^1.1.3" "react": "18.2.0"

body:
> Hi Guys, I tried all the solutions that were added in this discussion, but I still find myself having problems, Axios shows error 404

If you are getting a 404 response, that means the URL you are trying to make a request to does not exist.

Your code `api.post('/MissingPersonReport/findFace', ...)` is going to a server, and that server is replying that the request you are making has no endpoint configured (it is "404 - NOT FOUND").

So check your server logs to see what is happening.  Check your configuration of `api` to validate the host and port of the API server.

Your code is attempting to call:

```
https://api-abcd-prd.azurewebsites.net//MissingPersonReport/findFace
```

Is that the URL you expect it to be?

I also see in your JS console output:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/65d5689d-d217-4078-827d-12cb870f6d28)

So maybe you don't have your `api` configured correctly?  Why is `192.168.15.101:8081` showing up?
author: gregfenton

body:
> > Oi Guys, eu tentei todas as soluções que foram adicionadas nesta discussão, mas eu ainda me vejo tendo problemas, Axios mostra erro 404
> 
> Se você está recebendo uma resposta 404, isso significa que o URL para o qual você está tentando fazer uma solicitação não existe.
> 
> O seu código `api.post('/MissingPersonReport/findFace', ...)`está indo para um servidor, e esse servidor está respondendo que a solicitação que você está fazendo não tem nenhum endpoint configurado (é "404 - não FOUND").
> 
> Então, verifique seus logs do servidor para ver o que está acontecendo. Verifique a sua configuração de `api`para validar o host e a porta do servidor da API.
> 
> Seu código está tentando chamar:
> 
> ```
> https://api-abcd-prd.azurewebsites.net//MissingPersonReport/findFace
> ```
> 
> É essa a URL que você espera que seja?

body:
https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/d1178cad4c4fe21be3d36a5a89c1590f474b4d89/lib/defaults/index.js#L44-L54

We had set `axios.defaults.headers.common['Content-Type'] = 'application/json';`

This will override the guessing behavior - it will take your FormData and JSON.stringify it to match your default.

In older versions, Axios would guess despite the set content type.

author: felixh10r

body:
We could potentially change the name to path, then make it not required, then this might make more sense, people can then implement `baseUrl` and leave out path. Does this sound like a better solution?

We can also use a mapper to allow some backward compatibility with url to start off with and then add warnings and remove.
author: jasonsaayman

body:
I think you're hitting all the right keys with this last comment @jasonsaayman 🎉 

Introducing breaking changes in a minor or patch version completely goes against the spirit of [semantic versioning](https://semver.org/), but moving this change to v1, it's precisely what major version changes are for.

Personally, first time I used an axios instance with a `baseUrl`, when I tried using `request`, I looked for `path` first when writing its config, so I'd personally agree that it makes more logical sense that way. I think Axios v1 should be more instance focused, it is really a much better design pattern and time-saver to have a single source of truth of the `baseUrl` and then just append a `path` to it when needed anywhere else in the code.

Also, in regards to allowing to use a mapper for backwars compatibility in v1, that's a good way to make adoption of v1 easier, which in the long run means less time maintaining v0 both for users of axios as well as maintainers.

Thanks for the awesome work @jasonsaayman 🙌🏼 😄

body:
Seems like there is a problem in isFormData function:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17779660/150581549-a9755126-dd07-4b1a-94f1-276b3b82db3e.png)

I am using axios in react-native project where:
```js
const form = new FormData();
toString.call(form) === '[object Object]'; // true for React-Native
toString.call(form) === '[object FormData]'; // true for Browser
(form instanceof FormData) // true for both platforms
```

author: alfianwahid

body:
This issue appeared after this change: https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/3342/files#diff-5dfe38baf287dcf756a17c2dd63483781b53bf4b669e10efdd01e74bcd8e780aR57

As was said above, it is because `Object.prototype.toString.call(data)` returns `[object Object]` rather than `[object FormData]`, at least in React Native (`data` being declared with `const data = new FormData();`). Seems to be related to an older issue in `form-data`: https://github.com/form-data/form-data/issues/396

I managed to fix it without downgrading by adding this to the beginning of the index file in the app:

```
import FormData from 'form-data';

FormData.prototype[Symbol.toStringTag] = 'FormData';
```

body:
Adding a note here on my previous comment https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/4412#issuecomment-1025506556 that might be useful for someone.
Recently I found that there is `FormData` available globally in React Native, so there is no need to use the [form-data](https://www.npmjs.com/package/form-data) dependency. Using the global one removed the need to use that `FormData.prototype[Symbol.toStringTag] = 'FormData';` workaround, since the `thing instanceof FormData` uses the same FormData and returns true here in `isFormData` - https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v0.27.2/lib/utils.js#L192

body:
Looking at the code, there seems to be a catch all in the XHR adapters https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/master/lib/adapters/xhr.js#L118

It's not there in the HTTP adapter which will give the correct error so you may want to switch to that

I think it's because the XHR spec doesn't give [any clue about the error](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/XMLHttpRequest/error_event), so I don't think it can be fixed. As your then dependant on the browser implementation to trigger the .onError only if HTTP code = 0, and it seems some will trigger this with codes in the 400 range

body:
Hello to Everyone.
I just want to let you know that after searching for a solution for two days, I was able to solve my error.
Since the proxy was the source of the issue, I must configure a proxy in the package.json file, and I had to follow these instructions in the function that used Axios:
`try { await axios.post("user/login", formData).then((res) => { console.log(res.data); }); } catch (error) { console.log(error.response.data.message); }`
and in package.json file need to add a proxy:
`"proxy": "http://localhost:<SERVER_PORT_NUMBER>"`
for better understand check this documentation [https://www.jamesqquick.com/blog/configure-proxy-in-react/](url)

author: kumarldh

body:
https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/4420#issue-1111893293

Probably the original issue posted here has to do with how CORS is handled by the browser. Looking at your network console the API endpoint is allowing your origin to make the request since OPTIONS request is successful and (taking a guess here) they are also succeeding with small file POSTS and not the bigger than `threshold size` ones.

That leads me to think that  the  content too large 413 is probably served by  (

1. a reverse proxy like Nginx  sitting in the middle ( which is where the response is originating from and not your API). Is it possible that you have not allowed the origin as a part of the `error` response(s) originating from Nginx.  If yes chrome is probably disallowing the response from being read by your app. You can resolve this by providing `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header as a part of the 413 response after which axios will receive the response ( you will need to have access to the reverse proxy or the likes in the middle to add this header). 

OR

2.   A middleware (in your API) that intercepts the request and `does not` set the 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin'  headers appropriately while providing the `error response`. If not present chrome is probably disallowing the response from being read by your app (The browser/chrome receives the entire response as can be seen from the network console).

body:
> ```
> add_header 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' '*' always;
> ```
> 
> It's not enough to add CORS handling to your application code in this case, since it's nginx that's responsible for setting the correct header here.

Pay close attention to this! The same happened for me for 413. I was getting CORS error plus 413, since I only had CORS configured on php side, not in nginx.

What happens is when nginx returns 413 (or other error codes that are not returned from your app), the request never hits your application code, such as php, and the CORS headers never get set. So you **need** CORS headers in nginx too in order to avoid CORS errors in the console that block axios from getting the actual error code. 

I first was getting a generic `Network Error` when only using CORS in php, after adding CORS in nginx, I got `Request failed with status code 413`, which is much more descriptive.

body:
I'm also observing a memory ramp up when issuing subsequent POST requests to **different** URLs. I boiled the issue down to the following problematic code:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9198957/159266183-548df106-1030-450f-bc3b-e186549de63c.png)

The memory just keeps ramping up... After no more POST requests are issued, the memory is released at some point, but it may take up to 10 minutes for it to take effect.

When all POSTS are issued to the same URL (fix `run` and `i` in the above code to constant values), the issue disappears.



I'm observing this on React-Native v0.64.3 and axios v0.25.0.


This is the ramp-up I observe:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9198957/159267297-5baea41d-eb6b-4541-8f6a-43e8dc8be192.png)

When POSTing to the same URL repeatedly, the ramp up (mostly) disappears:

author: jasonsaayman

body:
> we should add it by default if data is not in FormData format, and content-type is `multipart/form-data`

You miss the point. The transformation to the FormData instance is applied only if the payload **is a plain object** and content-type is `multipart/form-data`, not for any payload with this content header. It's a very important difference.
Any object passed as a payload to Axios must be serialized in some way, and by default it uses `JSON.stringify()` no matter what content type is set.

#### So the question is:
Do Axios users pass a **plain object** as a payload with `multipart/form-data` and actually expect the payload to be passed as JSON?
#### Most Likely Answer:
No. Passing JSON with `multipart/form-data` will break all the standards. But even if the user wants to do this, they can still manually use `JSON.stringify` before passing the payload to axios.

Therefore, I believe this change will not break existing user projects in any way.

And yes, the serialization quality of that helper is not good enough. I'm working on better implementation.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
@DigitalBrainJS I did not miss that point (see quote at the beginning of your comment 🙂). 
Your claim that currently JS object are serialized to JSON is a strong one, and it makes sense to be consistent. 

I thought we need to have a test if a user passes FormData current change doesn't affect it in any way. But I noticed a test you added in https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/4448 that solves this issue. 

I guess my only reservation currently is that at first we should not recommend it as the preferred method of choice in the documentation. Be more gradual. First see how it is accepted (No major issues/bugs) and only then recommend it as the first/default method.

body:
Okay look, this is not a support forum for people learning to code but an issue board for axios

Just do a console.log(`http://api.brainshop.ai/get?bid=${bid}&key=${key}&uid=${uid}&msg=${msg}`') and you'll get the url, paste it in the browser it'll give you an HTTP error 400 because params are malformed. likely because you need to use encodeURIComponent on them before putting them in the URL or better yet use axios to correctly encode them

```
await axios.get(`http://api.brainshop.ai/get, {params: {bid,key,uid,msg}})
```

body: Why is the "possible bug" label removed? Clearly the automatic proxy configuration is incorrect. If the protocol is HTTPS and the `HTTPS_PROXY` environment variable set, the client should use the tunnel protocol by sending a CONNECT request, wait to get a 200 OK back and then send the original request on the same connection.
author: Nevon

body: Is there an update for this issue? which axios version should we use to workaround?
author: Yueren-Wang

body:
> Is there an update for this issue? which axios version should we use to workaround?

This worked for me:



> I've also faced this issue. It seems hpagent package help to fix this issue.
> 
> Here is example of usage:
> 
> ```
> const axios = require('axios');
> const { HttpProxyAgent, HttpsProxyAgent } = require('hpagent')
> 
> const httpAgent = new HttpProxyAgent({
>     proxy: 'proxy url'
> })
> const httpsAgent = new HttpsProxyAgent({
>     proxy: 'proxy url'
> })
> 
> const instance = axios.create({ httpAgent, httpsAgent });
> 
> instance.post('http://example.com')
> ```
author: srdi

body:
If anyone is interested in trying out HTTPS-over-HTTP support in Axios, feel free to check https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/5037. The implementation has some limitations (no redirects) but maybe it still fits your use cases. Would love to hear back from you!

Edit: Not directly related to this issue, but please note that there is currently (as of 1.1.2) some trouble around a breaking change in the proxy configuration: https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/5079

body: Yes, you can pass in a working agent implementation yourself. The problem is that Axios currently has a built-in implementation that is broken, so if you have the HTTPS_PROXY environment variable set it will fail by default.
author: Nevon

body:
Yes @Nevon, but i think axios could use proxy-agent internally. There is no need to have a built-in implementation of this.

Even if the proxy is not very difficult to understand, there is a lot of little details that can lead to multiple kind of bugs.
author: sebaplaza

body:
Is there any progress on this issue? 

- Axios not properly supporting https requests over a proxy prevents many depending products from working properly.
- In my case, I can not upgrade to a recent release of n8n because they dropped the alternative library on the step to 1.x.

Please fix this as this plays a central role in corporate environments.
author: cnlab-software-ag

body:
I faced the same problem and solved it using https://github.com/gajus/global-agent

It works with with all http client libraries that internally use [http.request](https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#http_http_request_options_callback), including axios.

author: jasonsaayman

body:
We ran into the same issue. Corporate proxy is advertised via env vars `https_proxy` with `http://` schema and explicit port `80`.

We use axios to fetch a binary in a npm install script (by coincidence also from Github). The installation fails with `read ECONNRESET`.

When we remove the explicit port from `https_proxy`, fetching works.

In the same shell with the same env vars, `curl` can fetch the resource in both configurations, with and without port.
author: filex

body:
I'm having the same issue as well. The corporate proxy for my company is on port 3128. When I'm making an API call, I'm getting `ECONNRESET` with a 400 response. Using Postman and CURL, I'm able to hit the API endpoint and obtain results.

Are there any workarounds for this? I'm using mailgun.js library that uses axios under the hood. I'm hoping I won't have to create a PR for mailgun.js just to fix this issue.

As an additional data point, the [mailgun.js NPM package](https://github.com/mailgun/mailgun.js) is using Axios 1.3.3.

-- Update --

I'm hoping to create a PR that will fix this issue, but I'm struggling on the code base to find where axios picks up on `http_proxy`, `https_proxy`, and `no_proxy` environment variables. I found the PR that adds support for `no_proxy` environment variable, but that was back in 2018 prior to version 1.x release. The code base has significantly changed sinced then.

I would appreciate it if anyone can point me in the right direction.

-- Update 2 (August 24, 2023) -- 
It turns out Axios v1.x has updated its code base to use another library called [proxy-from-env](https://github.com/Rob--W/proxy-from-env). The `getProxyForUrl()` function will tease out the environment variables. Testing Axios independently and running it on the NodeJS debugger within VS Code, the environment variables seem to get picked up which confuses me even more. The version of Mailgun for the project I'm working on is v7.0.2 which uses Axios 0.27. When I tried that code base, the debugger also seemed to have picked up on the environment variables. 

I tried explicitly adding a `.env` file and ensuring `dotenv` package is installed and configs imported into the application, but mailgun.js is still giving me the `ECONNRESET` response. 

I'm at a loss at this point.

body: Sorry I forgot to follow up with this one. I did quite a bit of work trying to get this working, but realized during testing that the HTTP library's request does in fact check other addresses including ipv4 and ipv6. I'm pretty sure that the HTTP library does not use the happy eyeballs algorithm under the hood, so this could still be implemented into Axios. However, it seemed redundant to me since all IPv4 and IPv6 addresses are checked anyways.

author: Sashank999

body:
I'll try to explain it since I've come across the same problem:

If you use axios inside a sandboxed environment, like with [vm2](https://github.com/patriksimek/vm2), the check at https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/master/lib/defaults/index.js#L24 fails because `process` is a mocked object which returns a standard `[object Object]` as its prototype. Due to this, no default adapter is created and app crashes.

I'm not sure why the extra check on the process prototype is needed, the issue that introduced it is 4 years old and I can't seem to get the reason: https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/1284.
In any case, I believe the check can be freely removed now because the condition has been moved after the XMLHttpRequest check, so there is no risk of loading anything wrong.

The problem can be bypassed temporarily by doing this:

```js
axios.get(url, {
   adapter: require('axios/lib/adapters/http')
})
```
author: reegodev

I can craft a PR with the check removed to see if all tests pass.
author: reegodev

body:
> I'll try to explain it since I've come across the same problem:
> 
> If you use axios inside a sandboxed environment, like with [vm2](https://github.com/patriksimek/vm2), the check at https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/master/lib/defaults/index.js#L24 fails because `process` is a mocked object which returns a standard `[object Object]` as its prototype. Due to this, no default adapter is created and app crashes.
> 
> I'm not sure why the extra check on the process prototype is needed, the issue that introduced it is 4 years old and I can't seem to get the reason: #1284. In any case, I believe the check can be freely removed now because the condition has been moved after the XMLHttpRequest check, so there is no risk of loading anything wrong.
> 
> The problem can be bypassed temporarily by doing this:
> 
> ```js
> axios.get(url, {
>    adapter: require('axios/lib/adapters/http')
> })
> ```

This solution was no longer adapted in axios 1.x. axios 1.x changed the logic here and added the `exports` field to package.json.

I asked the author in the vm2 repository and this is its response: https://github.com/patriksimek/vm2/issues/193#issuecomment-1365112963.
author: Y-qwq

Due to this, no default adapter is created and app crashes.

In any case, I believe the check can be freely removed now because the condition has been moved after the XMLHttpRequest check, so there is no risk of loading anything wrong.

Hello @jasonsaayman , I crafted a [PR](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/5792/files) to migrate the environment judgment logic to `knownAdapters`, it would enable the developer to always get the correct adapter.
If you think it makes sense, could you address it when you have time.

body:
After code debugging, the code of axios is no problem, it will always construct this value: `config.data = "{}"`.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28223260/162655923-52f36c18-fd13-44c7-bfef-b2a267817964.png)


The problem is that this is a get request, and the content in data is automatically ignored by the browser. But the server doesn't.

So this becomes a browser's own implementation issue.

So this question should need to be closed?

see:
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/978061/http-get-with-request-body

body: I'd rather recommend to use [`form-data-encoder`](https://github.com/octet-stream/form-data-encoder) package to do so. That way Axios will have support for `multipart/form-data` encoding in general, regardless of what package you choose - either formdata-node, or formdata-polyfill, or something else (except for form-data package, because it's not spec-compliant).

author: verhovsky

body:
> Most likely in the near future we should replace the default form-data module with formdata-node, or create our own simple stream encoder, but writing it in ES5 is painful.

Hmm. I think if you implement Blob support in Axios for Node.js adapter it should be relatively simple, because you can just convert FormData to blob like this: https://github.com/jimmywarting/FormData/blob/master/formdata-to-blob.js

To support Blob, however, Axios need to be able to read both ReadableStream and Readable (in case if somebody will use `fetch-blob@2.x`) produced by `Blob.prototype.stream()` method,  because other methods (`.text()` and `.arrayBuffer()`) would not be memory efficient.
author: octet-stream

body:
It is relative straight forward to create a NodeJS stream using: `node:stream.Readable.from(blob.stream())` if you still depend on that nodejs stuff. the fetch spec have recently changed to be acceptable of asyncIterator to support both whatwg & NodeJS streams, think you should support async iterators too...

- This tiny "formdata to blob" stuff that i have built is also what Deno and node-fetch is using in there impl.
- undici use [parts](https://github.com/nodejs/undici/blob/7d9fd51e7e35d5beb5db4f319132d757cd3aa696/lib/fetch/body.js#L86) of it (the iteration that yields all chunks)

I think we could start with adding support for Blob's as an acceptable data payload...

---

On the other hand: if we switch the adapter to use built in fetch in NodeJS v18 or using Undici or node-fetch
then it would be relative simple to support both blobs and formdata and just let this slide in as it can handle it for you so that you do not have to encode formdata or do anything special to support blobs either...
using fetch have also the benefit to make it work in service worker too... and cloudflare/Deno

maybe ppl should be able to bring in there own fetch impl if they wishes. Maybe it's time to ditch the old `node:http` & `node:https` core adapter module.

body: We will be releasing a v1 after 0.27, this should then fix the issues people have with versnioning. However please keep in mind that when releasing unde v0 we are in accordance with SemVer allowed to make breaking changes as we have in the past.
author: jasonsaayman

body:
Hmm. But minor unstable versions (before v1.0.0) are intended for breaking changes. Only patch versions <v1.0.0 should ensure that there are no breaking changes, so they may consist of new features and/or fixes.
Following npm version resolving rules (https://semver.npmjs.com/): 
^1.2.0 - will select 1.2.0, 1.2.1, **1.3.0, 1.3.1**, and so on
while the same minor operator ^
^0.2.0 - will select only 0.2.0, 0.2.1, 0.2.2 but not **v0.3.0** since it can consist breaking changes.

body:
The below change was introduced in v0.27.0:

> Removed functionality that removed the the Content-Type request header when passing FormData (https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/3785)

Now axios forcibly sets `content-type` header for requests with `FormData` payload.
Having possibility to send `FormData` request without a `content-type` header should do the trick.

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39280438/fetch-missing-boundary-in-multipart-form-data-post

```js
axios.get("/api/resource%3AcustomVerb")

body:
> Nothing to do with Axios. The colon needs to be encoded...
> 
> ```js
> axios.get("/api/resource%3AcustomVerb")
> ```

@philBrown Are you sure? Source?

I'm not intimately familiar with the standards in play for this but the following suggest it is valid without encoding:

- Example URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Template:Welcome
- Use without encoding in [Google Cloud API Custom Methods](https://cloud.google.com/apis/design/custom_methods)
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2053132/is-a-colon-safe-for-friendly-url-use
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1737575/are-colons-allowed-in-urls

body: Actually, we did not change anything in working with FormData - we just added alternative shortcut methods and payload serialization to a FormData object when you set the `content-type` header to `multipart/form-data` for plain object payload. This is just another shorter way to submit a form without manually creating a FormData object. The old behavior should not be affected, you can still pass the FormData object manually. FormData submits fails were only due to two regression bugs in versions 0.25.0 and 0.27.0.

author: EugeniuszX

body: @Melber17 Why would a GET request require to send a `Content-Type` header? GET requests don't have a body and setting `data` for them seems strange to me. Maybe there is an issue with the backend where you're sending the requests?
author: mlazari

body:
@mlazari  I don't know much about the server, but the documentation says that some API calls require you to send data in a certain format as part of an API call. By default, all API calls expect input in JSON format, however, you need to tell the server that you are sending the payload in JSON format.
And to do this, you must include the **Accept => application/json** and **Content-Type => application/json** HTTP headers on every call.
author: EugeniuszX

body: @Melber17 Yeah, but how do you send json with a GET request? It makes sense for a POST request, since you can send the json data in the body, but GET requests can't have a body as far as I know. Accept => application/json makes sense since that tells the server which content type the client can understand / expects in the response.
author: mlazari

body: I could be wrong, maybe it makes sense to have Content-Type for a GET request in some situations though... I see https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec14.html#sec14.17 mentions: "The Content-Type entity-header field indicates the media type of the entity-body sent to the recipient or, in the case of the HEAD method, the media type that would have been sent had the request been a GET."
author: mlazari

body: Here is an interesting article on this topic: https://evertpot.com/get-request-bodies/
author: mlazari

body: @mlazari  thanks for the information 👍. I think it's worth closing the issue. The problem is in the API I use

body:
I've experienced the same issue which seems to happen intermittently.

The request is made, but then it fails to receive the full response and no error is thrown - the process just hangs indefinitely.

Tried debugging on a patched axios 1.6.2 with a bunch of console.logs inside lib/adapters/http > req = transport.request(options, function handleResponse(res){}):

This is a get request and the response is gzip encoded.

```
res.headers['content-length']; // 19521

...

let totalResponseBytes = 0;
streams[0].on('data', (chunk) => { 
totalResponseBytes += chunk.length
console.log(`on data event: ${chunk.length} bytes received. Total ${totalResponseBytes} bytes of expected total ${responseLength} bytes` received.)
}
```
``// on data event: 16384 bytes received. Total 16384 bytes of expected total 19521 bytes received``

The stream fails to then receive any further chunks. When setting a timeout in the axios config this also never seems to trigger if the first chunk has already been received (seems to be expected behavior per #5886)

If I set ``onDownloadProgress: (e) => {}`` in my axios instance then it works fine:
``// on data event: 16384 bytes received. Total 16384 bytes of expected total 19521 bytes received``
``// on data event: 3137 bytes received. Total 19521 bytes of expected total 19521 bytes received``

So for the timebeing a workaround seems to be setting an empty onDownloadProgress config option.

body: @MattiasMartens Your cross-origin request is so-called [_simple_](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/CORS#simple_requests). As such, it doesn't (and shouldn't) give rise to a preflight request.
author: jub0bs

body:
> @MattiasMartens Your cross-origin request is so-called [_simple_](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/CORS#simple_requests). As such, it doesn't (and shouldn't) give rise to a preflight request.

I see, can I take it then that the fault here lies with Node (or possibly Chrome) for rejecting a request because of invalid CORS authorization when it shouldn’t need CORS authorization in the first place?
author: MattiasMartens

body: @MattiasMartens Neither Node nor Chrome is at fault here. If I understand your code correctly, you're only including the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header in responses to `OPTIONS` requests. That cannot work for clients that send `POST` requests; that header will need to be present in their responses too for things to work properly. Anyway, you're almost always better off using an existing middleware library/module (Express.js has [one](https://expressjs.com/en/resources/middleware/cors.html); use it) than re-implementing CORS "by hand".

body:
I think it's intentional because if the object is an AxiosError it doesn't have response.data, because of that it shouldn't be able to access the response.data object, because of that it's unknown

Here's the definition of type unknown in the official typescript documentation [Unknown type definition](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/functions.html#unknown)
author: VictorAugMiner
martinfoakes

body: +1 to the above. This is preventing an upgrade to 0.27.0 for me. All online resources point to the above error handling logic mentioned by @martinfoakes as standard practice but it no longer works with this new type definition. Downgrading to 0.26.1 fixes the issue.

body:
> Can confirm the above works. But I had already a check like below, which is not working anymore (meaning throwing Typescript error). Shouldn't it work too?
> 
> ```js
>  if (axios.isAxiosError(e)) {
>    console.log(e.response.data.message)
>  }
> ```

Not anymore, as now in  `v0.27` the `AxiosError` definition has changed from `AxiosError<T = any, D = any>` to -> `AxiosError<T = unknown, D = any>`. Which means that even after the check of `axios.isAxiosError(e)`, `e` will still be treated as type `unknown`, as the generic class type variable `T` is now default set to `unknown`

body:
Without redirects enabled, I have no idea what is causing this problem (follow-redirects has a streams backpressure problem).
Try to replace Axios with [dev/null stream](https://github.com/thlorenz/dev-null) and limit its speed with https://github.com/alxhotel/speed-limiter or a similar library. Maybe your stream is not respecting the overflow/high watermark value of the stream buffer.
P.S. The pipeline helper is a better approach to connecting your streams than pipe 

yourStream -> throttledStream -> devNullStream;

author: jasonsaayman

body:
Workaround: to fix the bug, we can add `maxRedirect: 0` option. 

```js
const myCustomAgent = new https.Agent({ timeout: 200 })

axios.get('https://google.com:81', { httpsAgent: myCustomAgent, maxRedirect: 0 })
```

That will force to use `https` from nodejs module instead of `https` from follow-redirects module and avoid the bug. 

**Watch out**, if you need to follow redirections this can be breaking for your application.

(See code: https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/lib/adapters/http.js#L269)

body:
For the time being, use `follow-redirects === 1.13.3`. 

You can pin the version using `resolutions` in package.json.

```json
  "resolutions": {
    "follow-redirects": "1.13.3"
 }
```

if you're using `yarn` or `pnpm`, it's good to go and for the case of `npm`, it needs [some extra work](https://www.npmjs.com/package/npm-force-resolutions).

Then you can set `timeout` on your `httpAgent`/`httpsAgent`.

author: khashayar-bo

body:
@khashayar-bo yes. It was not easy. There are two main issues. A recent change to the browser security specifications means that the browser will not store the session cookie provided by the back-end if:

1. The domain is localhost and:
1.1 The FQDN does not contain at least two dots (e.g. "fe.app.localhost" is good, "fe.localhost" is bad), 
1.2 AND / OR The protocol is not HTTPS

This makes it much harder to have a local dev environment.

The reason it works via POSTMAN is that POSTMAN is not a browser and so ignores that security specification. The reason it (at time of writing) works on Firefox is because they have not yet implemented this new specification.

References:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1134290/cookies-on-localhost-with-explicit-domain/1188145#1188145
(see comment on answer here) https://stackoverflow.com/q/69231649/2448430

This is [preventing me from supporting Axios 1.x](https://github.com/jamesmbourne/aws4-axios/issues/701) in https://github.com/jamesmbourne/aws4-axios/ as the AWSv4 signature relies on the final URL.

In previous versions I bolted together `buildURL`, `combineURLs`, and `isAbsoluteURL` but now these are not exported from the module.

I understand the reason for this in not wanting to have any internal change result in a breaking change, but knowing what the final URL Axios is going to make a request to is crucial.

body: The issue is that axios is setting a wrong `Content-Type` header if you don't pass it explicitly. See https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/4823#issuecomment-1224615032
author: mlazari

body: @mlazari we explicitly set the `'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'` but we still have the issue.
author: Nantris

body:
@Slapbox That doesn't change anything since you set the same value as the default. I think you must use `'Content-Type': 'multipart/form-data'` when you use FormData. From my debugging if you send FormData with the default `'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'` it doesn't make the request at all on Android and `XMLHttpRequest.onerror` is called here: https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v0.27.2/lib/adapters/xhr.js#L116 which causes the rejection with "Network Error".

Note that if you use `FormData` from [form-data](https://www.npmjs.com/package/form-data) you'll likely need this workaround: https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/4412#issuecomment-1025506556. But recently I found that there is a global `FormData` available in React Native that you can use and that works without that workaround.

body:
On iOS this was working fine, but on Android it was immediately failing without reaching the server with a "NETWORK ERROR".

I did have setup correctly in the headers `multipart/form-data`.

I didn't want to downgrade anything to fix this, i**f you are like ME** this how I fixed it, my mixing @mateusfg7 and @pnxdxt solutions.

There are my relevant libraries:

```json
    "axios": "^1.4.0",
    "expo": "~48.0.11",
    "react-native": "0.71.8",
```

And this is the code that I sent to my server:

```typescript
import mime from "mime";

// Upload is the document that you receive from the file-picker library in Expo
const uri = Platform.select({
      ios: upload.uri.replace("file://", ""),
      android: upload.uri,
});
 
const file = {
        name: upload.name,
        type: mime.getType(uri),
        uri,
}   
    
```

After this, it has started working flawlessly without downgrading anything. Cheers!

body:
I was getting the same error. Spent several days and a lot of time upgrading packages and trying all the solutions from issues and stackoverflow. Finally this was the mistake which I don't think is mentioned which even in the docs.

If we have a `FormData` object

```
formData.append("name", "abc")
formData.append("state", undefined)
formData.append("address", {
    addressLine1: "",
    addressLine2: ""
});

```
It will throw the same error since the `"state"` is `undefined` and `"address"` is an object. But `FormData` accepts only string or File or Blob. So this was causing the issue for me. Once I replaced `undefined` with `""` and `"address"` with `JSON.stringify(addressObj)` it started working.

Reference -> https://stackoverflow.com/a/72899049/5918233

It actually obscures the real errors (e.g. 407 as mentioned in post by @arossert)

Other errors are also incorrectly handled by axios and hidden, see https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/4888

It makes it hard to know what your API should do when we cannot properly differentiate cause of errors, and correct status code is not returned.

author: pincombe

body:
I recently fixed the issue. In our cases, it was usually related to WordPress with a cache plugin. If you try `curl https://your-domain`, the response is broken too usually.

The fix is adding this header in your request:
```
"Accept-Encoding": "gzip, deflate",
```

You can read this for more info:
https://github.com/louislam/uptime-kuma/issues/2253

Commit:
https://github.com/louislam/uptime-kuma/commit/3e68cf2a1cfab8cb8cc7969c915fba5535c7d827

author: colesiegel

body:
This cost me several hours, I finally narrowed down the problem for my case: The issue was the transfer-encoding. If set to `chunked` axios would throw the above error (on chunked there is no content-length, because its dynamic). The underlying reason looks to be a closed socket in the node http adapter. 

Parts of this might be due to my specific setup. I have Hoverfly as a mitm proxy between node and the real system. The real upstream system would return responses without chunked encoding, but Hoverfly would replay the request with chunked encoding.

So the request would work live, but not when replayed. It worked fine for a long time, the issue only appeared when upgrading from axios@1.4.0 to axios@1.6.2.

Now, I'm just speculating, but when comparing the original response and the replayed one I noticed a few things. Maybe the reason for the closed socket is an error in node itself, because the replay would work fine for curl & Postman.
In my specific example the data is interleaved with the connection being closed, I don't know enough about the standard to judge whether its the correct behavior, but I captured this with `curl -vvv`:
```
* TLSv1.2 (IN), TLS header, Supplemental data (23):
<Lots of JSON payload here...>* TLSv1.2 (IN), TLS header, Supplemental data (23):
* TLSv1.2 (IN), TLS header, Supplemental data (23):
* TLSv1.2 (IN), TLS header, Supplemental data (23):
* Closing connection 0
* TLSv1.2 (OUT), TLS header, Supplemental data (23):
* TLSv1.3 (OUT), TLS alert, close notify (256):<...Continued JSON payload>
```

So there is still some data arriving after the TLS connection has been closed. This is not the case without chunked encoding.
Could be completely unrelated, but maybe it saves someone else some time.

`baseURL` denotes the base part of the URL, i.e. without the relative path. For eg., say we have a URL `https://www.mydomain.com/images/event/1`, the `baseURL` here is just `https://www.mydomain.com`, and the relative path is `/images/event/1`.

With these being said, I don't really see a scope for improvement here?

body: The response headers were always available in previous versions. Is the expectation that response headers should be checked for undefined, or is this a recent breaking change?
author: wei-beamery

body:
I don't know about the history of the codebase. I just know that currently, you can get a value that'd be typed as `string`, although it could actually be `undefined` — so yes, the expectation is that response headers should be typed as `string | undefined`, to be more accurate.

I've created [a PR for this](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/4813).

author: mlazari

body:
Ok, so after a few hours of debugging, I finally found what's wrong - it's the `Content-Type` request header.
If you don't set it explicitly, then the default value is set here to `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` -> https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v0.27.2/lib/defaults/index.js#L10 and that apparently makes the request fail on Android, but not on iOS.
Setting the `'Content-Type': 'multipart/form-data'` explicitly fixes the error.
It was working in 0.26.1 because this code was removing the `Content-Type` header: https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v0.26.1/lib/adapters/xhr.js#L32

author: yh0x

body:
I was getting the same error. Spent several days and a lot of time upgrading packages and trying all the solutions from issues and stackoverflow. Finally this was the mistake which I don't think is mentioned which even in the docs.

If we have a `FormData` object

```
formData.append("name", "abc")
formData.append("state", undefined)
formData.append("address", {
    addressLine1: "",
    addressLine2: ""
});

```
It will throw the same error since the `"state"` is `undefined` and `"address"` is an object. But `FormData` accepts only string or File or Blob. So this was causing the issue for me. Once I replaced `undefined` with `""` and `"address"` with `JSON.stringify(addressObj)` it started working.

Reference -> https://stackoverflow.com/a/72899049/5918233

author: m0x61h0x64i

body:
I had the same problem with an API that returned data as Json. Data was returning without any problems in Postman. When I looked at the difference, I noticed that postmande assigned the value `"Cookie": 'AspxAutoDetectCookieSupport=1'` to the cookie after sending the request.

Maybe you might have a similar situation. You can find out by trying it in Postman.

author: arananegra

body:
> Axios Version: 0.27.2

It seems to be a version mismatch. You are looking at the documentation for Axios 1.0.0-alpha.1, where this feature is implemented, but in fact, you are using v0.27.2. The `postForm` method only supports multipart encoding in version v0.27.2.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12586868/177887519-5b39a13a-1148-4602-bd87-85077bfe6d9a.png)

[Sandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/axios-application-x-www-form-urlencoded-cwnli9?file=/src/index.js)

author: yunusyuksel

body:
I agree we need a connection timeout to axios. This provides greater control on connections.

Our use case is we have a Lambda making an HTTP request and we need to have diff timeouts for connection and response.

I tried the suggestion on https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/1739#issuecomment-769148019 but couldn't make it work (Axios `1.4.0` on Node `18.14.2`). But even with that suggestion, that's work around IMO, since one would expect that sort of control on a HTTP library.

author: DigitalBrainJS

body: I see, Is it stable to use alpha version on production?
author: ammar91

body: Alpha is not inherently stable, although this also applies to pre-1.0.0 versions that people have been using in production for years. In other words, the alpha label is a sign that there are no guarantees yet that the new functionality does not have significant bugs, or that the new API will not be changed. However, we always try to avoid changing the API.

author: MASSKAgithub

body:
**Solution:**
You only get this error when you change a supported proxy from "http" to "https".
So if you wrote "https://" change it to "http://". Or vice versa.

const axios = require('axios-https-proxy-fix');

body:
I observe a weird behavior. If I include `'accept-encoding': 'gzip, deflate, br',` in the request headers, the package `request` is able to pipe the whole file but axios is not. 

```js
const proxyRequest = request({
        url: '<url>',
        headers: {
           'accept-encoding': 'gzip, deflate, br'
       }
});
```
returns the full content:
```js
function o(){var n=document.createElement("script");} .....
```

but axios returns only a part of it:
```js
function o(){var n=document.createElement("scri
```

The reason seems to be the gzip header. If I remove that header axios is able to return the full content. Any Ideas ?
author: yvz5
yvz5

body:
> The problem was that axios was trying to unzip the stream because `decompress` is by default `true`. setting this value to `false` solved my problem.
> 
> ```
>  // `decompress` indicates whether or not the response body should be decompressed 
>  // automatically. If set to `true` will also remove the 'content-encoding' header 
>  // from the responses objects of all decompressed responses
>  // - Node only (XHR cannot turn off decompression)
>  decompress: true // default
> ```

I had exactly the same issue, but your solution helped me to fix it! Thanks @yvz5 !
author: MateuszKikmunter

body:
the line ` // - Node only (XHR cannot turn off decompression)
` from the docs suggests that even with `decompress: false` troubles may happen in he case of an XHR

author: martin-aloux

body:
In my condition, I was using axios.get() in node.js server, I previously set HTTPS_PROXY environment in my ~/.bashrc, it seems axios read that HTTPS_PROXY config when I triggerd a axios.get() on a https request.

Found this proxy environment issue in the log which shows in the proxy bracket.

Solution: add {proxy: false} in the config of axios.get(url, config)
`
const res = await axios.get(url, { proxy: false })`

Code:

                'method': 'POST',

                'apns-push-type': 'background',

            axios.post(url, { proxy: false }, { headers: headers })

body:
In our case, we are using Docker and using localhost doesn't work on our end.
The solution is that you can use the container name of the webserver (nginx) instead of localhost.
If you're using apache as your web server, use apache instead of nginx.

await axios.get('http://nginx/api/v1');

author: colesiegel

body:
Aparently, [this solution](https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/4406#issuecomment-1113367903)

```js
axios.post(url, formData, {
  headers: { 'Content-Type': 'multipart/form-data' },
  transformRequest: formData => formData,
})
```

Specifically this line:
`transformRequest: formData => formData,`
worked for me.

It makes sense since it assumes data transformation but then returns the same value🤔

body: So I just ran into this myself. I believe `error.response` only exists when you get a response from the server. If, for example, the error is `ECONNREFUSED` because the server isn't running, the `response` won't be present because there was no response.

Following up on this example, why are common error codes such as `ECONNREFUSED` or `ENOTFOUND` missing from the static readonly properties of AxiosError?

I committed https://github.com/WillianAgostini/axios/commit/2d7277ed795e6b114d4176e783d861c11869db5f to customize protocols on xhr adapter.

author: WillianAgostini

body:
Any reason why the test case of @realityfilter is not enough? Because this is not a capacitor-specific problem. All requests with a different URL scheme (protocol) are impacted, so the test should only check the following things:

- 1) When `registerProtocols` is **not** set, expect the error to be `ERR_BAD_REQUEST` + `Unsupported protocol xxx`
- 2) When `registerProtocols` is set, expect the error **not** to be `ERR_BAD REQUEST` + `Unsupported protocol xxx`

Any thoughts on this?

body: In production I got the same issue. The cookie was not set by the browser, I tryed a lot of possible solutions but what solve my problem was change the domain name. I deployed my API as server.mydomain.com and my frontend was anotherdomain.app and CORS was properly configured. I was able to send request to 'open' routes, but when I tried to login the cookie was ignored by the browser. So, when I changed the frontend to mydomain.com the cookie was set perfectly.

author: chapeee

body:
👋 Hey everyone,
I was also struggling with same issue, and now I found the answer, I hope this will fix your as well.

- Server side
    - Use cors with following configuration
    - CORS config: `app.use(cors({origin: <your_client_url> , withCredentials: true})`
    - Sending cookie: 
    `res.send(<cookie_name>, <cookie_value>,`
                  ` { httpOnly: true,`    // true if don't want to access cookie via js on client side
                    `secure : true,`       // mandatory with sameSite:none property
                    ` sameSite: "none",`  // mandatory to set if client and server are on different domains
                    ` maxAge: <your_desired_cookie_expiry_time>,`
                    `  path: "/"`  // must set path "/" here
                `  })`

- Client side
    - Create axios instance
    - `export const baseAPI = axios.create({`
	`baseURL: `${API_URL}`,`
	`withCredentials: true,`   // mandatory to set true
         `});`

Try to keep it simple, and avoid being redundant.

- Are you developing locally or in different domains? 
- How do you know your API is blocking the connection? have you tried with other client and getting a different result? eg postman, swagger.
- Are you logging your API responses before its way back to see the real status code?
- 
This doesnt seem like axios fault, looks like we are trying to find the status code on the wrong place of the request.

body:
Axios (XMLHttpRequest) cannot get the status code if:
- a network error has occurred (on TCP/IP layer)
- browser prevented JS from reading the request due to cross-domain request policy (CORS) or Mixed Content policy for HTTPs resources
- the request was canceled/aborted/closed outside Axios (by browser, ad blocker, server-side)
author: DigitalBrainJS

body: I had this issue when making a request to a dotnet API.  Noticed that `UseCors()` was at the bottom, moving to the top before all other middleware fixed the issue for me.
author: burt-tcc
GitHubington

body:
If you're using .NET API, you need to ensure that .UseAuthentication() is after .UseCors(), because it is a cors issue that breaks the pipeline before the authorization (where the step returns 401) .

If you want to make certain that the problems is the axios library, try to test a fetch() request direct from DevTools console. If the problems persists, is not axios problem, but a pipeline configuration problem on server side.

body: As @rodrigo-web-developer mentioned for .NET, for me using a spring backend, the problem was that by sending the response in the first filter, the cors header did not apply. I fixed this by adding the headers by hand like described here before sending the 401 https://stackoverflow.com/questions/16351849/origin-is-not-allowed-by-access-control-allow-origin-how-to-enable-cors-using

author: syedzubair74

body:
I recently had this issue and did a lot debugging about it.
In the end it turned out that the problem lies in the back end side.

Your server should set `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` or any other valid value in it's response headers when it's returning an unauthorized 401 response.

Usually they set this CORS values for successful responses, but omit them in error responses, Thus this issue happens.

*Hint for people with less knowledge about CORS issues:
In order to make sure that this is the issue, send your api without authentication headers in terminal with `cUrl` for example.
If you get 401 error (and not some arbitrary error from server), then probably everything is ok in front side and your server should set correct CORS headers in it's error responses.

body:
> I don't know how it happen, but add `type="button"` can solve error. But i think it should be working with out that attribute.

By default, when you click a `<button>` control, Firefox will submit the form after running any click handler. Firefox **will not wait** for requests in the click handler to complete, but will terminate any outstanding XHR/fetch request when it navigates. You can avoid this issue in your form using either:

* `<button type="button">`
* `<form onsubmit="return false;">` (possibly useful if the user presses the Enter key in a text input)

The other option is for the click handler script to ends with `return false;` to cancel form submission.

body:
I came across a similar issue today, I am using next 14 (app router) with Axios interceptor.

my Axios interceptor intercepts requests from client and server components, as people have shared that actions must not be mixed with simple functions, as behind the scenes they are making a post request to an API route internally.

whenever you are writing such code you must know in what contexts this code will be executed, if you are using it in a server context make sure you call them before the streaming starts, if the stream has already started you won't be able to manipulate request headers, as headers are already flushed!

body:
While downgrading axios solves the issue, it is not recommended. What I did was to patch axios using @osielmesa [PR](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/5152).

How to patch:
1. Make the changes according to the [PR](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/5152).
2. Install `patch-package` if you have not already done so.
3. Run  `npx patch-package axios`. Note that if you have not done Step 2, you will be prompted to install `patch-package`

Hopefully, this resolves the issue for us while we await axios to fix it from their end or approve and merge the [PR](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/5152)

body:
How about this?

# Curl command for illustration only
For illustration ... a curl command that returns with my personal GIT Access token a list of commits when searching the axios repo. As I said its just for illustration. If you want to execute the curl command you need to update the `my_token` value in the command. 

Request:
```
curl -v -s -X GET -H "Accept: application/vnd.github.v3+json" -H "Authorization:token my_token" https://api.github.com/search/commits?q=sort:committer-date-desc+merge:false+repo:axios/axios&per_page=50&page=1
```
Response looks normal as I get a list of commits.


# Axios v1.0.0-alpha.1 

## Steps/Run
* rm -rf node_modules
* npm install axios@v1.0.0-alpha.1 
* vi test.js
* copy/paste this into test.js
```
const axios = require('axios');

const config = {
    headers: {
        Authorization: 'token my_token',
        Accept: 'application/vnd.github.v3+json'
    }
}

axios.get('https://api.github.com/search/commits?q=sort:committer-date-desc+merge:false+repo:axios/axios&per_page=50&page=1', config).then(function (response) {
    // handle success
    console.log(response);
})
    .catch(function (error) {
        // handle error
        console.log(error);
    })
```
* Update `my_token` with your GIT Access token in above code and save.
* node test.js

Response looks normal as I get an array of commits back.

# Axios v1.0.0

## Steps/Run
* rm -rf node_modules
* npm install axios
* use same test.js as above
* node test.js

## Response
```
AxiosError: Request failed with status code 404
...
...
...
  data: {
      message: 'Not Found',
      documentation_url: 'https://docs.github.com/rest'
    }
```

body:
This might be an oversight but in v1 we have been a lot stricter with our `URL` parsing. The URL is for your URL free of any query params. Thus we have `params` as part of the config.

If you use a URL like this `http://test.example.com?test=1` we will be stripping out the `?`. Please use the params to set query params.

body:
Same here.
Tests are broken on v1.0.0 (node 16) because query parameters are no longer working.

Since most APIs, including [nodejs](https://nodejs.org/api/url.html#class-url) include the search parameters in the URL object, this change is miss-leading in my opinion.
Especially since there is no warning [in the readme](https://github.com/axios/axios#request-config) about it.

Also, it is very strange to remove the `?` character but not the actual parameters behind it if the `url` configuration param actually contains a query string... this is asking for trouble, I think.

author: Jannchie

body:
Came here from this [StackOverflow question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/73956043/axios-keeps-removing-question-mark-for-query-string/73956062#73956062).

The [docs](https://axios-http.com/docs/example) still says that `?` can be used in URL string, and using `params` config is optional.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/193136/194097817-d37a8665-ae56-4f89-a6bd-0ede19b984ef.png)

To resolve the discrepancy, either the library can be updated to support query params in URL strings (like in v0), or the docs can be updated to match the current v1 behavior.
author: dtinth

body: As I see no good reason for the New behaviour I would recommend to restore the one from v0 🙈
author: SteveW94

body:
Path including Query String is considered URL (URI) in HTTP semantics

https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#page-16

This changes break any application that accept URL as user input and pass directly as URL to axios.get(), post() and others.
Axios should support URL scheme according to HTTP semantics to conform with specifications and better user experience.

In case user supply query_params it should append to URL with & or ? as neccessary.
author: sfalpha

body: @jasonsaayman seems like PR #4852  should be reverted for now. Axios should definitely be able to parse params from a query string. Currently, these changes do more harm than good, since the problem they solve is not critical, but only potentially possible and has existed for years. This should be carefully considered from all points of view before such incompatibilities are introduced. Another, more thoughtful solution should be found.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
Clearly, this is a bug in Axios. `new URL(…)` has no problem parsing query params:

```ts
new URL('https://api.example.com/path?bug=axios')
URL {
  href: 'https://api.example.com/path?bug=axios',
  origin: 'https://api.example.com',
  protocol: 'https:',
  username: '',
  password: '',
  host: 'api.example.com',
  hostname: 'api.example.com',
  port: '',
  pathname: '/path',
  search: '?bug=axios',
  searchParams: URLSearchParams { 'bug' => 'axios' },
  hash: ''
}
```

body:
No, this is not fixed by #5030, **it's a complete different problem**.

People who are affected by this issue want to import utils from `axios/lib/...`, which is curretly forbidden by `package.json#exports`.

Currently `package.json#exports` only allow us to `import 'axios'`, but we are not just importing `axios`, we are importing `axios/lib/...`, and change exports of `axios` **won't help**.

The other issues closed by this #5030 are indeed importing from `axios`, they are fixed by #5030, but not this one.

I'm not sure if this is the correct suggestion since it's basically changing how Axios package is being shipped (And the effort is definitely huge). What if we modularize it and make the repository a monorepo approach similar to what [parcel](https://github.com/parcel-bundler/parcel) did?  
So we have scoped package like `@axios/core`, `@axios/helpers`, `@axios/adapters` and let them import from those instead? While the main Axios package itself only export the public API it intended, so we have a different package versioning for those scoped modules and the main Axios package.

body: @arthurfiorette we don't want to export everything else people will rely on code that can change without being documented as we can change underlying implementations without changing the public API. The only way I can see this working is we either export it and then whoever uses it does so explicitly at their own risk or we export only what is needed. author: Liubasara

body:
Hey @jasonsaayman. I know that importing code inside `lib` folder that are not part of the public API is strongly discouraged and it may break even in patch versions. 

The main reason we import things inside the `lib`/related folders is because we are trying to do something that axios does not support yet or reusing axios's logic in another part of our codebase.

The code is in OUR node_modules and just disallowing us from accessing it WILL not prevent us from using it. It is going to just create another bigger problem as, to solve our problem, we will copy/paste axios source code, which is much worse than our code breaking when upgrading axios, because it creates duplicity anyway and even future critical problems axios may fix won't reflect on our copy-pasted code.

And even if we achieve to import axios internal content without copy-pasting functions, I am sure that the code written to access it is more error-prone than just hypothetic incompatibilities axios may bring with a patch/minor version. 

I am strongly in favor of exporting everything axios has, even if it is going to be under a `internal`  property or similar names. 

I know that creating a issue to export `X` functionality will leave axios with much more control of what APIs you should care about or not, but in this issue/code/release meantime, the end-user will already have copied the function into his source code and probably will never look at it again, which leverages us to the same prior problem of axios fixing critical stuff and it not reflecting everywhere.

Happy to discuss more, also if you want, I can create another issue.

body: Sorry to add a "+1" here, but this is very important, IMO. I've dozens of axios calls across my app, and if the remote server returns anything but a 200, I see an error in my logs, I get an error in my error service, but I have no idea where it occurred. Right now, I can catch the error, and throw a new one, but then I lose some of the fidelity data from the HTTP response object. This was supposed to have been fixed in v1, so at first I thought I was doing something wrong, but I'm glad I found this issue to know that it wasn't just me. I also tried the `axios-better-stacktrace` package and even that did not work for me. This was supposed to be fixed multiple times, and is such a fundamental feature of this type of system library.

author: phloose

body:
Everything seems to be ok. Starting from v1.0.0, Axios switched to ESM modules, but for backward compatibility, we had to add two more prebuilt bundles to the `/dist` folder:
- browser ESM bundle
- node.js common js bundle for use with require

It's just the package size, not the library size.

body:
I am experiencing the same issue with axios version 1.0.0.

In plain node (ts-node) headers are returned correctly, e.g. this code returns correctly:

```ts
  const result = await axios.get("https://github.com")
  console.log(result.headers.server)
```

the output is ```GitHub.com```

however in jest, this code fails:

```ts
describe("Testing axios against external service", () => {

    it("should return headers ", async () => {
      const result = await axios.get("https://github.com")
      expect(result.status).toBe(200)
      expect(result.headers.server).toBe("GitHub.com")
    })

})

```

The ```result.headers.server``` is undefined.

In my investigation/debugging and tracing it boils down to this function:

https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/7a06a3616f34b50093ced773ec12ac7ab19e0a38/lib/utils.js#L129-L136

Specifically this line:

https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/7a06a3616f34b50093ced773ec12ac7ab19e0a38/lib/utils.js#L135

The response.headers in normal node are correctly identified as plain object, however in jest it does not identify them. 

It seems that in jest the prototype is different from the standard Object prototype, although I cannot find where they differ.
author: risa

body: And I am not sure what is exactly the purpose of comparing the prototypes, so I do not feel confident enough to propose a patch here.

body:
Unfortunately, this still does not work for me.

Background:  My React app is based on the latest production version, which includes react-scripts@"^5.0.1".  This inherits jest@"^27.4.3", which does not like libraries that only have ESM modules.  The errors I get are at compilation time when I try to run my tests, like this:

```text
    Jest encountered an unexpected token

    Jest failed to parse a file. This happens e.g. when your code or its dependencies use non-standard JavaScript syntax, or when Jest is not configured to support such syntax.

    Out of the box Jest supports Babel, which will be used to transform your files into valid JS based on your Babel configuration.

    By default "node_modules" folder is ignored by transformers.

    Here's what you can do:
     • If you are trying to use ECMAScript Modules, see https://jestjs.io/docs/ecmascript-modules for how to enable it.
     • If you are trying to use TypeScript, see https://jestjs.io/docs/getting-started#using-typescript
     • To have some of your "node_modules" files transformed, you can specify a custom "transformIgnorePatterns" in your config.
     • If you need a custom transformation specify a "transform" option in your config.
     • If you simply want to mock your non-JS modules (e.g. binary assets) you can stub them out with the "moduleNameMapper" config option.

    You'll find more details and examples of these config options in the docs:
    https://jestjs.io/docs/configuration
    For information about custom transformations, see:
    https://jestjs.io/docs/code-transformation

    Details:

    /Users/craigmcc/Git.Craigmcc/library/client/node_modules/axios/index.js:1
    ({"Object.<anonymous>":function(module,exports,require,__dirname,__filename,jest){import axios from './lib/axios.js';
                                                                                      ^^^^^^

    SyntaxError: Cannot use import statement outside a module

       6 | // External Modules ----------------------------------------------------------
       7 |
    >  8 | import axios, {AxiosInstance} from "axios";
         | ^
       9 |
      10 | // Internal Modules ----------------------------------------------------------
      11 |

      at Runtime.createScriptFromCode (node_modules/jest-runtime/build/index.js:1728:14)
      at Object.<anonymous> (src/clients/Api.ts:8:1)
```

I've also tried newer versions of Jest, but they do not solve the problem either.  Nor do the "transformIgnorePatterns" suggestions in previous comments.

In the dist directory of this Axios prerelease, it looks like you've created an axios.cjs file, but it's in a "node" subdirectory.  I've seen other libraries where this is in a "cjs" subdirectory, and/or there is some magic in package.json saying where to look.

author: oscarnovember

body:
I was having this exact same problem with Create React App, where none of these solutions helped. I finally found a way mocking the axios module would work with Jest.

I had to manually create an axios.js mock file following the Manual Mock directions https://jestjs.io/docs/es6-class-mocks

It looks like 
```
const axios = jest.fn().mockImplementation(() => {
	return {}
})

export default axios
```

I then imported axios in my test file and did `jest.mock("axios")`
After that, my tests ran fine.
I hope this can help anyone else struggling with this bug

body:
Here is the fix that finally works for me with Create React App. In your Jest config object in `package.json`, add a `moduleNameMapper`:
```json
 "jest": {
    "moduleNameMapper": {
      "^axios$": "axios/dist/axios.js"
    }
}
```
Make sure you use the specific regex `^axios$` or other packages that have `axios` in the module name may fail.

Versions for reference:
```
"jest": "^29.3.1"
"react-scripts": "^5.0.1"
"axios": "^1.2.2"
```

author: RyanJSmillie

body:
Given the amount of time that has gone on since my original bug report, and the fact that the only potential workarounds have all been just that -- workarounds (even in my experiments are only sketchily working for me) -- I'm giving up on Axios.  The native Node and browser implementations of the fetch APIs have satisfied my requirements, without this kind of hassle, so it's time to reduce dependencies in my various apps.  One app at a time.

FWIW, the React version I originally reported this for (18.2) is still current, which therefore means it will apply to anyone who uses Create React App (CRA) and even the current version of Axios (1.4), today.

Truth be told, this does make me a little sad ... Axios was the first HTTP API that worked well for me on both the client and server sides.  But time marches on.  And I cannot afford to be stuck in a world where there is (say) a security vulnerability in Axios 0.whatever, that is only fixed in Axios 1.whatever, which would mean invalidating a very large portion of my client side tests.

I think that casting to AxiosHeaders and using 'set', is a much better and sustainable solution then suppressing an unspecified error on the next line.

author: pieterh

body:
I don't stand with casting. Casting, for me, just means you lie to the compiler. 

`@ts-expect-error` works differently from `@ts-ignore`, if the problem has been fixed, `@ts-expect-error` will raise an error since it's not being used. Unless this bug has been fixed and another issue comes up in the same version, and at the same line of the code, then it's another story. And I personally don't think this will happen.
author: xsjcTony

body: Interesting insight. Thanks. The casting is indeed a temp solution.
author: pieterh

body:
> I don't stand with casting. Casting, for me, just means you lie to the compiler.
> 
> `@ts-expect-error` works differently from `@ts-ignore`, if the problem has been fixed, `@ts-expect-error` will raise an error since it's not being used. Unless this bug has been fixed and another issue comes up in the same version, and at the same line of the code, then it's another story. And I personally don't think this will happen.

I think casting is okay in this case. We know `config.headers` is AxiosHeaders but the problem is TS thinks it is undefined.

Solution? Define it with the correct typing.

```
config.headers = { ...config.headers } as AxiosHeaders
```

Then you can freely assign anything to your headers now.
```
config.headers.set(key, value)
```

¯\_(ツ)_/¯

Overall example code:
```
const api = axios.create({ headers: { Accept: 'application/json' } })

api.interceptors.request.use((config) => {
  const token = '_sample_token'
  config.headers = { ...config.headers } as AxiosHeaders
  config.headers.set('Authorization', `Bearer ${token}`)
  return config
})
```

Cheers 🍺

```
const headers = {"HeaderName": "HeaderValue"}

To do this:
```

body:
A major breaking change to a project this big is not acceptable. This should have been handled differently:

* Deprecate the functions MONTHS in advance, giving warnings when used. Finally in the breaking version, the breaking change should give a verbose error message when used.
* Use a major version for breaking changes (i.e. `1.0.0` -> `2.0.0`
* Provide a migration guide to fix these changes.

author: yousef-iskandar

body:
> I think anyone who is having this problem should really get some consultancy about how to deploy applications to prod.
> 
> I'm not trying to be condescending. I'm telling this because all your deployments are wide open to a supply-chain attack which is even worse than having your prod broken.

More specifically:

- **Test your package upgrades _before_ deploying them to production.** Anyone in this thread complaining about production being broken clearly didn't do this. Outsourcing your QA efforts to your customers is not ideal.
- **Pin your package versions**. Just because your project currently works with one version of the package does not mean it will work with another.

body:
Since the latest versions, there are two groups of users with different ways importing axios
1. `const axios = require('axios')`
2. `const axios = require('axios').default`

IMO, it would be ideal if we can find a way to support both styles, if not, we have to choose one and declare the other one invalid (which I think is `1` because `2` is stated in README)

body:
Thanks for all the feedback everyone, also some great pointers here on how to manage your own software. On the way Axios should work when using it with Common JS it should always be:

```js
const axios = require('axios')
```

I will work to make sure this is always the case. I will also update all the docs etc ASAP. Lastly, I will fix the problems that seem to now exist when using Type Script. I know this was a rough release, but I will endeavour to make them more smooth from here on.
